lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Baghdad. The House of Wisdom. A city that had decided, for a moment, that the past was worth paying for.

lines.speaker: Ibn al-Nadim
lines.text: You want to know why al-Mamun sent his agents into Byzantium for manuscripts? I'll tell you the real reason. Not the noble one.

lines.speaker: Ibn al-Nadim
lines.text: Always. An astrologer told him a Greek philosopher would defeat him in a dream. He woke and ordered translations. Statecraft dressed as scholarship.

lines.speaker: Mele
lines.text: So the whole translation movement — Euclid, Galen, Aristotle — started because a caliph had a bad night's sleep?

lines.speaker: Ibn al-Nadim
lines.text: The motive was fear. The result was a library. History rarely asks which was which.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The political anxiety that launched the work outlasted the caliph who felt it. The translations remained. The fear, eventually, did not.

lines.speaker: Ibn al-Nadim
lines.text: Or by ambition. The difference is smaller than scholars like to admit.
